segments.title: Arleigh Burke as CNO force builder
segments.summary: The lecture argues that Arleigh Burke's deepest legacy was not just destroyer aggression, but his political skill as CNO in building a deterrent and globally visible US Navy.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke presents Burke as a rare officer who combined tactical reputation, strategic judgment, and senior political skill. The lecture moves from his destroyer career and staff work into the CNO period, where Burke pushed nuclear carriers, guided-missile surface ships, and Polaris submarines as tools for war fighting, conflict management, and visible deterrence.

evidence.note: The opening says Burke is usually remembered as a destroyer officer, but frames his CNO tenure, political skill, and ability to win funding and procurement battles as the central theme.
The destroyer command section traces Burke's rapid wartime rise, aggressive decision style, Blackett Strait lesson, Cape St. George context, and selection as Marc Mitscher's chief of staff.
The CNO force-building section links Enterprise, Albany, Leahy, Bainbridge, and other missile or nuclear ships to Burke's attempt to create a fleet able to win wars and win peace.
The Polaris section presents Burke's strategic-deterrence case for roughly forty ballistic-missile submarines and frames that force as central to deterring conflict while managing peace.
